nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs, often on jetty pillars, sponges, and other sheltered hard structures; benthic at depths from the shallows down to around 70 m, though typically encountered above 20 m

diet.notes: Ambush predator that lures prey with a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy esca shaped like a small fish or shrimp. Generally feeds on fish and crustaceans, and has been reported to engulf prey nearly equal to its own body length. New specimens typically only accept live feeder shrimp or small marine fish at first; many can be trained over time to take frozen krill, silversides, or pieces of marine fish from tongs. Feeding every 3–5 days is generally advised, as overfeeding has been linked to bloat and food refusal.

goodWith: large peaceful fish too big to be swallowed

imageCandidates.notes: Bernard DUPONT; 2048x1380. Vivid orange color-phase Commerson's frogfish, whole animal in frame among red whip corals, sharp and highly striking.
Diego Delso; 5296x5296. Deep-red color phase, sharp frontal view of the whole fish; high resolution.
Steve Childs; 2370x2577. Dark olive/black color phase matching the 'Black Angler' trade name, mouth open, whole animal in situ; sharp.

summary: The Black Angler is a large, slow-moving frogfish that hunts by dangling a fleshy lure to draw prey within striking range. It can swallow tankmates close to its own body length, so it is best kept alone or with fish too large to be at risk. Intermediate experience is recommended.
careNotes:
The Black Angler (Antennarius commerson) is the dark color phase of Commerson's Frogfish, one of the largest Antennariidae and a famously cryptic ambush predator. Body coloration is highly variable across the species — cream, pink, yellow, orange, red, green, brown, and black phases are all reported — and individuals can shift hue over a period of days to match their surroundings. The 'Black Angler' trade name refers specifically to dark-pigmented specimens; husbandry needs are identical across color morphs.

The fish hunts by wiggling a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy lure (esca) that mimics a small fish or shrimp, then lunging forward with an extremely fast strike to vacuum prey into its mouth. It has been documented swallowing prey roughly equal to its own length, so any small fish or motile invertebrate in the same tank is generally at risk.

Generally considered reef-safe with respect to corals, though contact with stinging anemones (especially elegance anemones) and certain large-polyp stony corals has been reported to injure its scaleless skin. The Black Angler is decidedly not safe with small fish, shrimp, or ornamental crustaceans. Strong conspecific aggression is well documented — wild individuals are solitary, and females have been reported to attack or even consume males outside of brief spawning encounters.

Feeding is the principal husbandry hurdle: new specimens commonly refuse anything but live prey, and starvation is a real risk if a transition to frozen foods is not established. Many keepers train the fish to accept frozen silversides or krill offered from tongs by mimicking the movement of live prey.

No venomous spines are documented for this species (unlike the related Striated Frogfish), and it is generally regarded as harmless to humans.
breedingNotes: Oviparous. Wild spawnings produce a ribbon-like or veil-like gelatinous egg raft that drifts in the water column until hatching.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous. Captive breeding is uncommon; eggs are released in a gelatinous ribbon or veil that drifts in the water column. Pair maintenance is difficult given documented female aggression toward males.
